_disclaimer: Educational decision-support reference only. Not a medical device. Final triage decisions are always made by a licensed clinician.
_sources: Acuity levels follow the Emergency Severity Index (ESI) v4 five-level framework; red-flag patterns are adapted from ESI and Manchester Triage System concepts. This is a curated demo subset, not a complete or certified protocol.

1.label: Resuscitation
1.description: Immediate life-saving intervention required (e.g. cardiac arrest, apnea, severe shock).

2.label: Emergent
2.description: High-risk situation, severe pain/distress, or dangerous vital signs. Should not wait.

3.label: Urgent
3.description: Stable vital signs but likely needs multiple resources (labs, imaging, IV).

4.label: Less urgent
4.description: Stable, likely needs a single resource (e.g. one X-ray or sutures).

5.label: Non-urgent
5.description: Stable, needs no emergency resources (e.g. prescription refill, minor complaint).

chest pain.esi_criteria: Cardiac-sounding chest pain (pressure, exertional, radiating, with diaphoresis/nausea) is ESI 2 until ruled out. Abnormal vitals or distress escalate toward ESI 1. Reproducible, pleuritic, or clearly musculoskeletal pain in a well-appearing patient with normal vitals may be ESI 3.

red_flags.sign: Crushing/pressure pain radiating to arm, jaw, or back
Chest pain with diaphoresis, nausea, or dyspnea
Sudden tearing pain radiating to the back
Chest pain with syncope, hypotension, or SpO2 < 92%

shortness of breath.esi_criteria: Respiratory distress with low SpO2, accessory muscle use, or inability to speak full sentences is ESI 1-2. Mild dyspnea on exertion with normal SpO2 and no distress is ESI 3.

red_flags.sign: SpO2 < 92% on room air
Unable to speak in full sentences / accessory muscle use
Sudden onset with chest pain and leg swelling
Stridor, drooling, or lip/tongue swelling

abdominal pain.esi_criteria: Most undifferentiated abdominal pain needing labs and imaging is ESI 3. Signs of peritonitis, GI bleeding, or unstable vitals escalate to ESI 2 or 1.

red_flags.sign: Rigid abdomen with rebound tenderness
Severe pain with hypotension or tachycardia
Vomiting blood or black/tarry stools
Pregnant with sudden severe lower abdominal pain

headache.esi_criteria: Sudden 'thunderclap' headache, focal neuro deficit, or altered mental status is ESI 1-2. Chronic/recurrent headache resembling prior migraines with normal exam is ESI 3-4.

red_flags.sign: Sudden 'worst headache of life' (thunderclap)
Headache with fever and neck stiffness
Headache with focal weakness, slurred speech, or vision loss
New headache with confusion or seizure

fever.esi_criteria: Fever with signs of sepsis (hypotension, tachycardia, altered mental status) is ESI 1-2. Well-appearing adult with isolated fever and stable vitals is often ESI 3-4. Immunocompromised or very young/old patients escalate.

red_flags.sign: Fever with hypotension or SpO2 < 92%
Fever with petechial/non-blanching rash
Fever with confusion or lethargy
Fever in an immunocompromised or post-chemotherapy patient

stroke symptoms.esi_criteria: Any acute focal neurological deficit (face/arm/leg weakness, speech difficulty, vision loss) is a time-critical emergency and is ESI 1-2. Resolved or chronic deficits with normal vitals are lower acuity but still warrant urgent evaluation.

red_flags.sign: Facial droop, arm weakness, or slurred speech (FAST positive)
Sudden vision loss or double vision
Sudden severe dizziness with difficulty walking
Deficit with decreased consciousness

allergic reaction.esi_criteria: Airway involvement, breathing difficulty, or signs of shock indicate anaphylaxis and are ESI 1. Localized hives with normal vitals and no airway/breathing involvement are ESI 3-4.

red_flags.sign: Lip/tongue/throat swelling or difficulty breathing
Widespread hives with dizziness or hypotension
Known severe allergy with recent exposure

injury.esi_criteria: High-energy mechanism, uncontrolled bleeding, or unstable vitals are ESI 1-2. An isolated minor injury (small laceration, suspected simple fracture) in a stable patient is often ESI 3-4.

red_flags.sign: Uncontrolled bleeding or signs of shock
Head injury with loss of consciousness, vomiting, or confusion
Suspected spinal injury (neck/back pain after trauma, numbness)
Open fracture or obvious deformity with severe pain

mental health crisis.esi_criteria: Active suicidal/homicidal intent, agitation, or risk of harm requires immediate safety measures and is ESI 1-2. Stable patients seeking support with no acute risk are ESI 3-4. Always treat expressed intent conservatively.

red_flags.sign: Active suicidal or homicidal intent or a plan
Suspected overdose or ingestion
Severe agitation or psychosis with safety risk

back pain.esi_criteria: Most back pain with normal vitals and no neurological signs is ESI 4-5. Neurological deficits or systemic red flags escalate sharply.

red_flags.sign: New leg weakness, numbness, or loss of bladder/bowel control
Back pain with fever or IV drug use
Sudden tearing back pain in an older patient

vomiting and diarrhea.esi_criteria: Signs of significant dehydration or shock (tachycardia, hypotension, lethargy) escalate toward ESI 2. A well-appearing patient tolerating fluids with stable vitals is ESI 4-5.

red_flags.sign: Signs of severe dehydration or hypotension/tachycardia
Blood in vomit or stool
Severe abdominal pain with persistent vomiting

general.esi_criteria: When the complaint does not match a specific protocol, base ESI on vital sign stability, distress level, and resources likely required. Any dangerous vital sign or altered mental status escalates to ESI 1-2.

red_flags.sign: Altered mental status / unresponsiveness
Any dangerous vital sign (e.g. HR > 130, SBP < 90, SpO2 < 92%, RR > 30)
Severe uncontrolled pain (>= 8/10)
